name: Matilda House
alias: Istana Woodneuk's quieter cousin · the Punggol mansion
body: A colonial bungalow built in the early 1900s and named for a man's late wife, it stood derelict in Punggol for decades while the district around it emptied of kampongs and then filled again with high-rise flats. The legend holds that the house was cursed, by a grieving son in some versions and a grieving mother in others, so that no one who tried to settle there would ever stay: doors slamming through the night, sleep broken without cause, the living quietly driven out. As a haunting it is gentler than most, a house that does not harm so much as refuse, turning away each new tenant until it is left alone again.

What makes it linger is its survival rather than its ruin. While other colonial houses fell to demolition or to fire, Matilda House was conserved, and the old dread now sits folded inside a modern condominium, the bungalow's white facade preserved at the development's edge like a tooth set in new gold. Residents pass it on their way to the pool and the car park; the curse, if it is a curse, has been zoned, landscaped, and given a heritage plaque, made to coexist with show-flats and morning joggers.

What remains is the strange courtesy of the arrangement, the way the city kept the haunted thing instead of clearing it, so that the story now has to be told in the present tense of property and management committees. The mansion endures as a preserved gatehouse and clubhouse for the flats behind it, lit softly at night, neither abandoned nor quite lived in, and the children who grow up beside it learn the legend the way they learn the names of the lifts: as a fixed feature of home, a haunting with an address and a postcode, waiting in plain sight at the front of the estate.
note: Matilda House is a documented conservation case: a genuine early-twentieth-century bungalow in Punggol that was gazetted for conservation and incorporated into a later residential development rather than demolished. That much belongs to the public record of architecture and heritage policy, and is the most reliable layer of the entry.

The curse itself, the slamming doors, the tenant who could never stay, and the competing son-versus-mother origin stories belong to local lore and ghost-tour retelling, and should be presented as such rather than as fact; avoid inventing specific names, dates, or events to dramatize it. The genuinely interesting thread is how a legend adapts to redevelopment instead of dying with the building: worth tracking is how residents and tour guides now retell the story now that the house is a conserved clubhouse fronting modern flats, and how proximity and ownership reshape a haunting that was born when the place stood empty.
